A lot of them are too heavily chiding you for not working hard enough and for other behavioral issues, right? And that'll scare them. I mean, people don't want to be told to work. So you've got to be to a certain point before I can really have that conversation with you. As a matter of fact, early on, so think about it like recycling stuff out of the info letter if we were sending faxes to newbies in the info business. They're saying, no good, right? Because early on what I want to tell you is this ain't a real business. All you need is a card table and a cat that'll lick stamps and you can sit on the beach with your laptop four out of five days and you can make a million dollars. When you're a little more ready, I'm going to tell you this is a real business and you got to shoot the cat and go get five employees. And so you've got to be real careful what you recycle, especially when I'm not doing the recycling. So because Heather, whoever's doing it, doesn't have that sensitivity that we're talking about here. So the raw material that she's given to work from is pretty limited. My early years in the Amway business, you know, Amway really, first of all, there's the business people think it is and the business it really is. And really there's about three different levels of the business it really is. And they're very good about only talking to rank and file distributors and not really revealing the five or six truths about what the business is until you've hit direct distributor volume three months in a row and then you get put in a room and told some of the truth because you're committed enough, you're not going to run screaming from the planet upon hearing it. And then you get told the rest of the truth after you've got six direct distributors and your diamond and you're kind of, you're making so much money you can't leave, right? And so there's a certain amount of that same thing in our businesses. And it's, see, it's a real tightrope of the newsletter because I got to talk to you, but I got to talk to that new person at the same time. But so the emails kind of need to be gentle. And I don't know how often we rerun the same ones either. I know we do. I imagine there's only 40 or 50 or 60 of them things and they're going around and around and around and around. It's what I would guess is happening. Yeah. Is it a year? I think so. Yeah. I mean they do, look, they drive a lot of traffic back to the Web Store, but they could definitely be a lot better. The Web Store is a very good business, very profitable business. But, and I'll take them just as an aside, and this is actually relevant to you for the Cal-O business. Really anybody that has members and then a bunch of leads is sort of the old pre-Internet rule. One of the good things about the Internet is the non-member, the non-customer didn't have much value. And in fact in the catalog business you had to purge them. So every so often you had to run the buy anything for a dollar or some whiz bang offer and if they didn't do that you had to take them off the list because the cost of communicating with them was too great. Well, now you can talk to them forever, right? And sort of one thing that is really surprised us about our business in the last five years is how much value there is in what I call the persistent non-member. For one reason or another they absolutely will not get in or stay in as a member. We have now, of course we force them now on the Web Store, if you go to the Web Store and you're not a member, you're hooked into free trial if you buy anything. They'll be in for one month past the free trial, drop out, buy something four months later. They'll be in again, they run out the back end again, but they keep buying. And the old rule was get rid of them, you know, because if they're not going to do the basic base thing you want them to do there's no point in having them around and that's definitely not true anymore. I mean actually our non-member list is actually now worth more in our member list, which is a dramatic reversal, you know, in business. Can you sign my seminar? Huh? Yeah. You can have a giant non-member seminar and then try to pull it back in. We've done it. Well we did it. Obviously you can leave it again. And that's one of the ways you get them. Because if they do come into a room that changes them dramatically. But sure our December event in LA last year was 60% non-members. September sales seminar was 40% non-member. Now Bill doesn't like to do those, but there's a very good and smart and practical reason, you know, to do them. But if you're an information marketer and you don't have a catalog site with multi-products, multi-priced and all that, see like you make your promotions go away, right? See I think that's half right. I think some of that should happen, but some of it should not. Some of those should be permanently available catalog items over here, essentially trotted out and put on special every so often via a promotion. For some of the price and then we have to push. Yeah. Yeah. I mean I think you leave a lot of money on the table and you cut off a path for somebody and that's maybe the only path that they'll take. Only a lot of our stuff is like the program starts on a date. Can we do people? Yeah. You're right. That's definitely not a premium point on the floor. Yeah. See and once you have it you also start to create product for it. So collections of old newsletters for example can be clumped together and made into a product that it doesn't justify its own promotion, but stuck up on a catalog site and you got anywhere, anyway, good to go, right? Pisa, what do you know? I got a lot of questions. The book that I gave you guys isn't like one complete newsletter so if you're reading it that way, I just threw a bunch of stuff together and printed it out for you. If you had any question about that.
